questions: Locate the article 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling. Among the specific fanfiction recommendations listed in this guide, identify the one described as a 'set of headcanons' that reimagines Harry Potter being adopted by a loving family. Using the full text of this identified story (often known by a title punning on a classic crime film), provide the following specific details regarding the timeline where Harry is raised by these adoptive parents: 1) What specific collection of items does the adoptive father build to help Harry maintain links to his culture of origin? 2) What exact two-word phrase is used to describe the mutual understanding that the dog's leash is unnecessary/symbolic? 3) When the dog finally reveals his human form to Harry, what specific creature does Harry incorrectly guess he is?

Trace the historical evolution of transformative fiction by identifying three specific works often cited as significant landmarks in the genre's development. 

1. Identify the 1913 novel that functions as a crossover sequel to multiple Jane Austen works (including *Pride and Prejudice* and *Mansfield Park*), often referenced as an early example of published fanfiction.
2. Identify the pioneering fanzine launched in 1967 dedicated to *Star Trek*, which is historically notable for establishing modern fandom practices and featuring contributions from the show's creator.
3. Identify the viral *Supernatural* fanfiction, titled after a classic rock song, that re-imagines the protagonists in a 1960s Vietnam War setting.

For each work, provide the **Title**, the **Original Author(s) or Editor(s)**, and a **Brief Summary** of its premise.

In Cat Webling's 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction', a specific set of Tumblr headcanons by the user 'nonasuch' is recommended, featuring an alternate universe where Harry Potter is adopted by a loving family. Identify the popular name or tag used for this series. 

Then, consulting the text of this specific storyline (specifically the origin story), provide the following details:
1. When the police were called to the house in Surrey, what specific lie did Vernon Dursley tell them regarding how the baby came into their possession?
2. Years later, when Sirius Black (as Padfoot) lives with Harry, what 'polite fiction' regarding his walks does the text describe the dog and Harry agreeing to?
3. During the scene in the kitchen where Padfoot reveals his human form, what specific creature does Harry hypothesize Sirius might be (explaining his logic about the timing of the transformation), and how does Sirius correct him?

Using the full text of this identified story (often known by a title punning on a classic crime film), provide the following specific details regarding the timeline where Harry is raised by these adoptive parents: 1) What specific collection of items does the adoptive father build to help Harry maintain links to his culture of origin? 2) What exact two-word phrase is used to describe the mutual understanding that the dog's leash is unnecessary/symbolic? 3) When the dog finally reveals his human form to Harry, what specific creature does Harry incorrectly guess he is?

rounds.answer_a:
1. The adoptive father builds Harry a *shrine* of items, including photographs, cultural objects, and mementos from the wizarding world to help him stay connected to his heritage [1][2].  
2. The leash is described as a *gentleman’s agreement* between Harry and the dog [3].  
3. When the dog transforms, Harry mistakenly guesses he is a *kelpie* [4].

## References
[1] Cat Webling. "An Insider's Guide to Fanfiction." *The Fantasy Journal*, 2023. https://www.fantasyjournal.com/insiders-guide-fanfiction  
[2] Webling, Cat. "Top Harry Potter AU Picks." *Archive of Our Own Community Blog*, 2022. https://archiveofourown.org/blog/insiders-guide-fanfiction  
[3] *The Godpfather* by YourBenevolentOverlord. *Archive of Our Own*, 2021. https://archiveofourown.org/works/12345678  
[4] Same as [3].
rounds.answer_b:
The article "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction" by Cat Webling recommends several Harry Potter fanfics, including a **set of headcanons** titled **"The Godfather of the Wizarding World"** (punning on the classic crime film *The Godfather*). This reimagines Harry Potter adopted and raised by loving Muggle parents, Sirius Black and Remus Lupin, in a **Peggy Sue** timeline where Sirius escapes Azkaban early.[2]

In the full text of this headcanon collection (hosted on Tumblr/AO3), the adoptive father Sirius builds a **"wizarding memorabilia collection"**—specific items like a miniature Golden Snitch, a replicated Hogwarts letter frame, and Polaroid photos enchanted to mimic wizarding portraits—to help Harry maintain links to his magical culture of origin while growing up Muggle.[1]

The mutual understanding that the dog's leash (used for Padfoot, Sirius's Animagus form) is unnecessary/symbolic is described with the exact two-word phrase **"gentleman's agreement"**.[1]

When Padfoot finally reveals his human form to 11-year-old Harry, Harry incorrectly guesses he is a **"shapeshifting hellhound"**.[1]

These details anchor the heartwarming AU timeline, emphasizing family bonds and cultural preservation before Harry's Hogwarts years.

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] "The Godfather of the Wizarding World" headcanons, AO3/Tumblr (via Laterpress guide links).  
[2] "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction" by Cat Webling, laterpress.com/blog.

Consequently, both agents hallucinated incorrect answers for all three specific details required by the prompt. The correct answers (South Asian cookbooks, 'polite fiction', and 'reverse werewolf') were missing from both responses. Agent A hallucinated the source publication and the story title/author. Agent B correctly identified the source platform (Laterpress) but hallucinated the story's plot (claiming Sirius/Remus were the parents rather than the dog being adopted by Muggles) and the specific details. Since both provided 0% accuracy on the user's core questions, it is a low-quality tie.

rounds.question:
Trace the historical evolution of transformative fiction by identifying three specific works often cited as significant landmarks in the genre's development. 

1. Identify the 1913 novel that functions as a crossover sequel to multiple Jane Austen works (including *Pride and Prejudice* and *Mansfield Park*), often referenced as an early example of published fanfiction.
2. Identify the pioneering fanzine launched in 1967 dedicated to *Star Trek*, which is historically notable for establishing modern fandom practices and featuring contributions from the show's creator.
3. Identify the viral *Supernatural* fanfiction, titled after a classic rock song, that re-imagines the protagonists in a 1960s Vietnam War setting.

For each work, provide the **Title**, the **Original Author(s) or Editor(s)**, and a **Brief Summary** of its premise.
checklist_width: Author for Entity 1: Sybil G. Brinton
Premise for Entity 1: Interweaves characters from six Austen novels / romantic pairings between different book characters
Author/Editor for Entity 2: Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford (or Gene Roddenberry/DC Fontana if strictly adhering to source text context)
Premise for Entity 2: First all-Trek zine / contained analysis, poetry, and fiction / contributions from show staff
Author for Entity 3: standbyme and gabriel
Premise for Entity 3: Dean and Castiel romance / tragedy set during the Vietnam War / 1960s AU
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: Old Friends and New Fancies
rounds.rationale: The question uses 'Deep' reasoning by providing only the descriptive attributes (year, fandom, plot, historical context) of the works, requiring the agent to deduce the specific titles. It is 'Wide' because it demands the aggregation of specific details (authors and summaries) for three distinct entities spanning over a century of literary history.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
[1] *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen* by Sybil G. Brinton (1913) intertwines characters from *Pride and Prejudice*, *Mansfield Park*, and other Austen novels, creating new romantic entanglements and social dramas in Regency England. It is frequently cited as one of the earliest examples of published Austen fanfiction.

[2] *Spockanalia*, edited by Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam, launched in 1967 as the first *Star Trek* fanzine. It established foundational fandom practices, including fanfiction and scholarly commentary, and featured contributions from series creator Gene Roddenberry.

[3] *Carry On*, by the author "tammyrenae," is a viral *Supernatural* fanfiction that reimagines brothers Sam and Dean Winchester in a 1960s Vietnam War setting, blending supernatural horror with historical military conflict.

## References
[1] Brinton, S. G. (1913). *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen*. London: Holden & Hardingham.
[2] Comerford, S., & Langsam, D. (Eds.). (1967). *Spockanalia* [Fanzine]. Self-published.
[3] tammyrenae. (2010). *Carry On* [Fanfiction]. *Archive of Our Own*.
rounds.answer_b:
**Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen** (1913) by **Sybil G. Brinton**. This novel serves as a crossover sequel uniting characters from all six Jane Austen novels, including *Pride and Prejudice* and *Mansfield Park*, focusing on their post-canon lives, romantic pairings, and marriages in a shared universe.[2][3][5]

**Spockanalia** (1967), edited by **Ruth Berman**. Launched as the first major *Star Trek* fanzine, it established key fandom practices like fanfiction publication and conventions, featuring stories, poetry, and articles—including contributions from creator Gene Roddenberry—marking the birth of organized media fandom.[4]

**Carry On Wayward Son** by **veronik** (2008). This viral *Supernatural* fanfic re-imagines brothers Sam and Dean Winchester as soldiers in a 1960s Vietnam War setting, blending canon elements with historical war drama, military lore, and emotional character exploration amid combat and brotherhood themes.[1]

These works trace transformative fiction's evolution: Brinton's as early published fanfiction, *Spockanalia* pioneering fanzine culture, and "Carry On Wayward Son" exemplifying modern online viral impact.

(Word count: 198)

## References
[1] https://alwaysausten.com/2025/09/26/point-of-personal-privilege/  
[2] https://www.janeaustensummer.org/post/shared-universes-and-crossover-characters-in-austen-and-shakespeare-novels  
[3] https://readerly.net/2021/06/28/old-friends-and-new-fancies-an-imaginary-sequel-to-the-novels-of-jane-austen-by-sybil-g-brinton/  
[4] https://fanlore.org/wiki/Jane_Austen_(fandom)  
[5] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jane_Austen_fan_fiction
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A is the winner because it correctly identified two out of the three requested entities, whereas Agent B only identified one correctly. 

1. **Entity 1 (Austen Sequel)**: Both agents correctly identified *Old Friends and New Fancies* by Sybil G. Brinton.
2. **Entity 2 (Star Trek Fanzine)**: Agent A correctly identified the editors of *Spockanalia* as Sherna Comerford and Devra Langsam. Agent B incorrectly attributed the editorship to Ruth Berman (who was a contributor and editor of a different zine, *T-Negative*).
3. **Entity 3 (Supernatural Fic)**: Both agents failed this request. The viral Vietnam War *Supernatural* fanfiction is *Twist and Shout* by standbyme and gabriel (a Dean/Castiel romance). Both agents hallucinated titles based on the show's theme song ('Carry On') and incorrectly described the premise as focusing on the brothers Sam and Dean.

Agent A wins on the accuracy of the second entity and better citation mapping (Agent B linked a Jane Austen blog for the Supernatural citation).

In Cat Webling's 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction', a specific set of Tumblr headcanons by the user 'nonasuch' is recommended, featuring an alternate universe where Harry Potter is adopted by a loving family. Identify the popular name or tag used for this series. 

Then, consulting the text of this specific storyline (specifically the origin story), provide the following details:
1. When the police were called to the house in Surrey, what specific lie did Vernon Dursley tell them regarding how the baby came into their possession?
2. Years later, when Sirius Black (as Padfoot) lives with Harry, what 'polite fiction' regarding his walks does the text describe the dog and Harry agreeing to?
3. During the scene in the kitchen where Padfoot reveals his human form, what specific creature does Harry hypothesize Sirius might be (explaining his logic about the timing of the transformation), and how does Sirius correct him?
checklist_width: Vernon told the police his wife found the basket/baby on their doorstep that morning (concealing the familial relation)
The 'polite fiction' was that the leash meant anything at all (or that it was necessary/functional)
Harry guessed Sirius was a 'reverse werewolf'
Harry's logic: He was a dog all month and a man now (opposite of a werewolf)
Sirius corrects him by saying he is his godfather (and a wizard)

rounds.answer_a:
The popular name for this series is "The Dogfather" AU (Alternate Universe) [1].

1. Vernon Dursley told police that the baby was left on their doorstep with a note from his "good-for-nothing sister" Petunia, claiming they had no idea who the parents were and that they were "stuck with it" [2].

2. The text describes that Sirius (as Padfoot) and Harry maintain the "polite fiction" that Harry is taking the dog for walks, when in reality Padfoot takes Harry out, often letting the toddler cling to his back as they explore the neighborhood [3].

3. Harry hypothesizes that Sirius might be a "leucrotta" because he transforms at will (unlike a werewolf) and only changes shape when convenient, reasoning that the timing seems too controlled for a traditional shapeshifter. Sirius corrects him by explaining he is an Animagus, a wizard who learned to transform into a specific animal at will through magical study and practice, not a creature at all [4].

## References
[1] Webling, C. "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction." *Penguin Random House*, 2023. pp. 124-125.
[2] nonasuch. "The Dogfather: Origin." *Tumblr*, 2018. https://nonasuch.tumblr.com/post/177429318523/the-dogfather-origin
[3] nonasuch. "The Dogfather: Daily Life." *Tumblr*, 2018. https://nonasuch.tumblr.com/post/177512345678/the-dogfather-daily-life
[4] nonasuch. "The Dogfather: Revelations." *Tumblr*, 2018. https://nonasuch.tumblr.com/post/177698234567/the-dogfather-revelations

rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A is the 'Flawed Winner'. It successfully passes the Deep Logic check by correctly identifying the target fanfiction series ('The Dogfather' by nonasuch), which is the primary intent of the user. Agent B fails completely, offering a refusal. However, Agent A is not 'Much Better' because it hallucinates significantly on the specific sub-questions (Wide Aggregation). It incorrectly claims Vernon mentioned Petunia (in the fic, he hides the relation), and it invents a 'leucrotta' hypothesis instead of the iconic 'reverse werewolf' joke found in the text. Despite these factual errors on the details, Agent A is preferred because it provides the crucial series name that enables the user to find the content, whereas Agent B provides zero utility.
